name: Canada
summary: The textbook English-native study→3-year PGWP→Express Entry (CEC)→citizenship pipeline, with dual citizenship retained both ways (Canada is on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list), five-year naturalisation at CLB 4, and a top-tier AI ecosystem (Vector, Mila, Amii); from 1 January 2026 a master's at a public DLI is exempt from both the study-permit cap and the PAL requirement. Tempered by a collapse in new study-permit approval rates (~30% in 2025 vs 51% in 2024) after the Student Direct Stream was cancelled, severe Toronto/Vancouver housing costs, a ~21% cut to permanent-resident targets (≈380,000/yr, 2026–28), and a cooling immigration-sentiment climate under Carney's minority Liberal government plus Quebec's Bill 21.

links.title: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada — official
EduCanada — Study in Canada (Government of Canada)

job-market.summary: Top-tier AI ecosystem (Vector, Mila, Amii) and a tech labour market at ~3.3% unemployment across Toronto/Vancouver/Montreal/Ottawa/Waterloo, with the PGWP open work permit removing LMIA friction for new graduates.

pros.text: Tech unemployment is ~3.3% (near full employment) and software/AI roles (NOC 21231/21232, TEER 1) are a confirmed Express Entry priority category for 2026; AI/ML skills are the fastest-growing demand in tech-hiring data.
Software-engineer pay in Toronto runs ~CAD 90,000–137,000 and AI/ML engineers ~CAD 85,000–150,000, with a ~35% machine-learning premium.
Multiple deep hubs (Toronto, Vancouver, Montreal, Ottawa, Waterloo, Calgary) can absorb both engineers, and the PGWP is an open work permit — no LMIA or employer tie.

cons.text: Direct employer sponsorship of a foreign worker still needs an LMIA; the smooth route runs through study→PGWP→Canadian-experience PR rather than offshore hiring.
A 2023–24 tech-layoff cycle and the 2025–26 immigration-cap climate have softened entry-level hiring at the margin.

links.title: Job Bank — AI (software) engineer wages, Canada (Government of Canada)
Robert Half — Software Engineer/Developer salary, Toronto (2026)

visa-access.summary: Lodgement and biometrics stay in Dhaka via VFS with ~8-week processing, and a master's at a public DLI is exempt from the 2026 cap and PAL — but new study-permit approval rates collapsed to ~30% in 2025 (from 51% in 2024) after the Student Direct Stream was cancelled.

pros.text: Applications are lodged online with biometrics at the VFS Canada Visa Application Centre in Gulshan, Dhaka (CAD 85, valid 10 years) — no travel to New Delhi; study-permit processing runs ~8 weeks.

From 1 January 2026 master's and doctoral students at public DLIs are exempt from both the national study-permit cap and the Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) requirement, removing the allocation bottleneck for this profile.

cons.text: New study-permit approval rates fell to ~30–31% in 2025 (from 51% in 2024) after SDS was cancelled (8 Nov 2024); the top refusal reasons are an officer being unconvinced the applicant will leave and unverified proof of funds.
The cancelled SDS removed the only fast-track; all Bangladeshi applicants now go through the regular stream with full genuine-student and source-of-funds scrutiny.
Proof of funds rose to CAD 20,635 living costs (up from CAD 10,000) plus full first-year tuition and return airfare, typically evidenced via a Canadian-bank GIC.

links.title: IRCC — End of the Student Direct Stream and Nigeria Student Express (official)
VFS Global — Canada visa biometrics, Bangladesh
IRCC — 2026 provincial and territorial allocations under the international student cap (official)

citizenship.summary: Five-year naturalisation (1,095 days' physical presence in 5 years) with a low CLB 4 language bar, dual citizenship retained both ways, and a top-10 passport.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Canada has allowed it since 1977, and Canada is on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality (apply for the DNC to avoid losing it).

Naturalisation needs 1,095 days of physical presence in the 5 years before applying (up to 365 pre-PR days count as half), language only at CLB 4, a knowledge test, and 3 years of tax filing — a trivial language bar for English-fluent engineers.
The Canadian passport ranks 9th on the Henley index with visa-free access to ~183 destinations.

cons.text: The 5-year/1,095-day clock effectively begins from PR (pre-PR time is capped at a 365-day half-credit), so citizenship trails PR by several years.
Reaching citizenship still depends on first securing PR through Express Entry, the binding gate (scored under PR Pathway).

links.title: IRCC — Canadian citizenship: who can apply (official)
Citizenship Act, RSC 1985, c. C-29, s. 5 (Justice Laws)

post-study-work.summary: The Post-Graduation Work Permit gives a master's graduate a 3-year open work permit regardless of programme length, with the field-of-study restriction lifted for degree graduates and each graduate spouse eligible for their own PGWP.

pros.text: Master's graduates qualify for a 3-year PGWP regardless of programme length (since 15 Feb 2024), and the field-of-study restriction was lifted for bachelor's/master's/doctoral graduates in March 2025.
The PGWP is an open work permit — any employer, any role, full-time, self-employment allowed — and each graduating spouse can independently hold their own 3-year PGWP.
The 3-year runway plus one year of skilled Canadian work feeds directly into the Canadian Experience Class for PR.

cons.text: Degree graduates must prove language at CLB 7 in all four abilities to qualify for the PGWP (since 1 Nov 2024) — easy for English-fluent applicants but a hard gate for others.
Conversion to PR is not automatic: it runs through competitive Express Entry CRS draws, and the STEM category has no guaranteed dedicated draws in 2026.

links.title: IRCC — About the Post-Graduation Work Permit (official)
IRCC — PGWP: who can apply (official)

spouse-family.summary: Where both spouses study they each hold their own permit and PGWP; otherwise a spousal open work permit is available because a master's of 16+ months qualifies, and children attend public school free.

pros.text: A master's programme of 16+ months keeps the spouse eligible for a Spousal Open Work Permit under the Jan-2025 rules; if both spouses study, each instead holds an independent study permit and 3-year PGWP.
Dependent children can accompany and attend public K-12 school free of charge during the study and work phases.
Each spouse has a self-contained study→PGWP→Express Entry route, so neither is locked into permanent dependant status.

cons.text: The Jan-2025 reform restricted spousal open work permits to spouses of master's (16+ months), doctoral or select professional students — shorter or college programmes no longer qualify.
From 4 March 2026 a spouse of a student in their final academic term is refused even on renewal, creating a tail-end gap.

links.title: IRCC — Changes to open work permits for family members of temporary residents (official)

msc-study.summary: World-class English-taught CS/AI master's (Toronto, Waterloo, UBC, McGill) with 24 hrs/week off-campus work and a standard programme qualifying for the maximum 3-year PGWP, though international tuition runs CAD 23k–35k/yr.

pros.text: Deep, top-ranked English-taught CS/AI catalogue — Toronto, Waterloo, UBC, McGill — with research-stream master's frequently funded and Waterloo-style co-op embedding Canadian work experience.
Off-campus student work was raised to 24 hours/week (from 20) effective Nov 2024, and a 16+ month master's qualifies for the maximum 3-year PGWP.
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is directly admissible at the graduate level subject to GPA and IELTS/TOEFL.

cons.text: International master's CS tuition is high — ~CAD 23,088/yr at Waterloo and ~CAD 34,900/yr at Toronto — and coursework (non-research) master's are largely unfunded.

links.title: University of Toronto — Computer Science graduate funding & tuition
IRCC — Work off campus as an international student (official)

pr-pathway.summary: A clear study→PGWP→Canadian Experience Class route reaches PR in ~3–4 years with only CLB 7 English, but Express Entry CRS draws are competitive and 2026–28 admission targets were cut ~21% to ≈380,000/yr.

pros.text: Study (≈2 yrs) → PGWP → one year of skilled Canadian work → Canadian Experience Class can deliver PR in roughly 3–4 years, a transparent points-based route.
PR language sits at CLB 7 (easy for English-fluent engineers) with no separate integration exam, and Provincial Nominee streams provide a backup.
Over 40% of 2025 PR admissions came from in-Canada temporary residents, and the government has explicitly prioritised in-Canada transitions.

cons.text: Express Entry CRS cut-offs for Canadian Experience Class draws have run high (frequently ~520–540), so PR is competitive even for two engineers and may need a PNP.
The 2026–28 Immigration Levels Plan stabilises PR at ≈380,000/yr — about 21% below earlier targets — and 2025–26 saw category churn (STEM dropped dedicated draws, agriculture delisted).

links.title: IRCC — Canada prioritizes top talent in 2026 Express Entry categories (official)
IRCC — Canadian Experience Class: who can apply (official)

income-cost.summary: Two tech salaries leave a comfortable surplus and remit easily, but Toronto and Vancouver are among the least affordable housing markets globally and the student phase is tight.

pros.text: A dual tech household (~CAD 180k–220k gross) nets a comfortable surplus at ~25–28% effective tax, and CAD repatriation to Bangladesh is straightforward.

cons.text: Housing dominates costs: a 1-bedroom in Toronto runs ~CAD 1,800–2,700/month, with a couple budgeting ~CAD 3,800–4,100/month all-in; Toronto and Vancouver are the most expensive Canadian cities.
The study phase is tight — ~CAD 23k–35k tuition plus ~CAD 24k–30k living per year — surmountable only with reserves and the 24-hour student-work allowance.

links.title: Mastersportal — Living costs & tuition for international students in Canada (2026)

healthcare.summary: Universal public healthcare once resident, with strong family benefits ($10/day childcare, generous parental leave), but newcomer coverage has gaps — Ontario students rely on mandatory UHIP and PGWP holders need a 6-month job for OHIP — and treatment waits are long.

pros.text: Permanent residents and citizens get universal public coverage; EI and CPP plus up to ~18 months of parental leave provide a solid safety net.
The Canada-wide $10/day childcare programme and the Canada Child Benefit materially lower family costs once settled.

cons.text: Coverage is patchy for newcomers: Ontario international students rely on mandatory UHIP (~CAD 792/yr) rather than OHIP, and a PGWP holder needs a 6-month full-time job to qualify for OHIP (BC/Manitoba cover immediately).
Treatment access is a known weakness — GP shortages and some of the longest specialist and surgical waits in the wealthy-OECD group.

links.title: Government of Canada — Health care in Canada (official)

culture-language.summary: English-native and highly multicultural with no language barrier for status, strong settlement services, and a large South-Asian presence — though immigration sentiment has cooled since 2022.

pros.text: English is the working and everyday language outside Quebec, so there is no separate language friction for work, PR or citizenship (only CLB 4–7).
Highly multicultural with IRCC-funded settlement services and large established South-Asian communities that ease adaptation.

cons.text: Public opinion has hardened — the share saying there is 'too much immigration' more than doubled to ~58% by 2024 (stabilising at ~56% in 2025) — cooling the welcome for newcomers.
In Quebec, French is essential for daily life and provincial services, narrowing the English-only option.

links.title: CBC — Popular support for more immigration has cratered
Government of Canada — Improve your English/French (settlement services, official)

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 2025: 13th, 0.79) and a peaceful, safe society (GPI 2025: 14th), tempered by rising hate-crime figures and Quebec's Bill 21 restricting religious symbols.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 13th of 143 (0.79); Global Peace Index 2025: 14th of 163 — stable courts, low corruption and generally low everyday crime.
Immigration enforcement is rule-bound with IRB appeal rights, and federal/provincial human-rights codes are broadly enforced.

cons.text: Statistics Canada has recorded rising hate crime, with anti-Muslim and anti-South-Asian incidents elevated; the 2017 Quebec City mosque shooting (6 killed) and 2021 London, Ontario family attack (4 killed) remain reference points.
Quebec's Bill 21 bars some public-sector workers from wearing religious symbols — a notable anti-discrimination carve-out affecting visibly Muslim residents.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025
Global Peace Index 2025 (PDF)

politics.summary: Very stable institutions under Carney's minority Liberal government (elected April 2025), but a sustained 2024–26 immigration tightening (study caps, ~21% PR cut, SDS/SOWP changes) and a hardened public mood make policy volatile for newcomers.

pros.text: Institutions are very stable: the April 2025 federal election produced an orderly transition to a Carney-led Liberal government, and skilled migration (Express Entry) remains fully operative with in-Canada transitions prioritised.
No far-right party governs; immigration scepticism sits with the mainstream Conservative opposition rather than an extremist bloc.

cons.text: Immigration policy has tightened sharply across stages since 2024 — study-permit caps, SDS cancellation, spousal-OWP restrictions and a ~21% cut to PR targets — under Carney's pledge to cap admissions at 'sustainable levels'.
Quebec's Bill 21 is provincial state targeting of religious symbols, and a cooled public mood (56–58% 'too much immigration') pressures further restriction.

links.title: 2025 Canadian federal election (overview)
CBC — Carney's immigration strategy amid a shift in public opinion

tax.summary: A moderate OECD tax wedge (~31%) with individual assessment that doesn't penalise dual earners and an in-force Canada–Bangladesh double-tax treaty, offset by no special expat regime and a high ~53.5% top combined marginal rate in Ontario.

pros.text: The Canada–Bangladesh double-taxation convention has been in force since 1985, giving credit relief and clean repatriation; the OECD single-worker tax wedge (~31%) is well below Germany's.
Tax is assessed individually (no joint penalty on the second earner), and the lowest federal rate was cut to 14% for 2026.

cons.text: Ontario's top combined marginal rate reaches ~53.5%, and there is no expat or inbound-worker tax relief regime of any kind.

links.title: Canada–Bangladesh income-tax convention (Treaty text, Government of Canada)
PwC — Canada: taxes on personal income

muslim-diaspora.summary: A large established Bangladeshi-Muslim community concentrated in the Greater Toronto Area, with abundant mosques, halal food and a rich South-Asian ecosystem — though rising Islamophobia and Quebec's Bill 21 temper the sentiment picture.

pros.text: ~75,400 Bangladeshis (2021 Census), the largest concentration in Toronto/GTA (Thorncliffe Park, Danforth, Mississauga), within a ~1.8m / 4.9% Muslim population — the second-largest religion in Canada.
Mosques and halal food are abundant across the GTA, set in a rich South-Asian ecosystem (Gerrard India Bazaar, Brampton) and federal multiculturalism/Charter religious-freedom protections.

cons.text: Anti-Muslim and anti-South-Asian incidents have risen (Statistics Canada hate-crime data), and Quebec's Bill 21 restricts religious attire in parts of the public sector — a localised but real accommodation gap.

links.title: Statistics Canada — Religion (2021 Census of Population)
Bangladeshi Canadians (community overview)
